tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is accurate identification and documentation of iron deficiency anemia as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States a clear primary diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia and explicitly links it to low iron/iron depletion.
Communicates the diagnosis to the patient using the phrases "iron deficiency anemia" and "low iron" (or clearly equivalent wording that includes both required terms).
Explains a plausible mechanism (insufficient iron intake/absorption or blood loss) and frames next steps to identify the underlying cause, especially occult blood loss if appropriate.
Mentions treatment at a patient-education level and includes the term "oral iron" when discussing management, without necessarily prescribing (since L1).

Performs medication safety reasoning before any prescribing: checks for significant drug interactions and contraindications are treated as critical (must not prescribe if unable to assess), and asks about allergies as a non-critical safety check.

Avoids unsafe or premature prescribing (e.g., starting anticoagulants, NSAIDs for presumed bleeding, or iron infusions) without appropriate indications; does not dismiss potential serious causes (e.g., GI malignancy) when risk factors are present.
Provides appropriate follow-up guidance (symptom monitoring, when to seek urgent care for red flags such as chest pain, syncope, melena/hematochezia) even if not using a follow-up tool.

minimal_paths.description: Identify iron deficiency anemia based on typical symptoms and supportive history; provide patient-facing explanation including low iron and oral iron education; outline evaluation for cause.
must_collect: dyspnea on exertion or reduced exercise tolerance
pica or restless legs (optional but supportive)
history of blood donation or recent bleeding
past history of anemia or GI disease (celiac, IBD)
red flags (syncope, chest pain, significant weight loss)
steps: Elicit key symptoms and bleeding risk/history (menses, GI bleeding, diet, pregnancy).
Synthesize findings and state diagnosis: iron deficiency anemia due to low iron.
Explain diagnosis in plain language including why low iron causes symptoms.
Discuss management at a high level including oral iron, expected timeline for improvement, and common side effects.
Recommend evaluation of the underlying cause (especially sources of blood loss) and outline follow-up/return precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis-first approach: agent states suspected iron deficiency anemia early, then backfills key history to confirm and to assess for bleeding sources and red flags.
steps: State leading diagnosis (iron deficiency anemia) as a working impression.
Ask targeted questions to confirm low iron etiology and assess bleeding sources (menses/GI) and risk factors.
Provide patient explanation including the terms iron deficiency anemia and low iron.
Discuss oral iron as the typical treatment and counsel on administration/side effects at an educational level.
Advise follow-up and evaluation for underlying cause, with urgent return precautions for red flags.

acceptable_variants.description: Differential-aware approach: agent briefly considers alternative causes of anemia (B12/folate deficiency, anemia of chronic disease, thalassemia) but concludes iron deficiency anemia as most likely and communicates accordingly.
steps: Gather symptom profile and relevant history (diet, bleeding, chronic disease, family history).
Briefly mention alternative causes of anemia and why iron deficiency anemia fits best.
Explain diagnosis using required phrases iron deficiency anemia and low iron.
Discuss oral iron as standard therapy and emphasize finding/treating the cause of iron loss.
Provide follow-up guidance and red-flag precautions.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying congenital factor XIII deficiency is the core L1 outcome; it explains delayed bleeding with normal PT/aPTT and guides appropriate specialist management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital factor XIII deficiency and explains that routine coagulation studies can be normal, including normal PT (and typically normal aPTT).

Elicits key bleeding history features consistent with factor XIII deficiency (e.g., delayed bleeding after trauma/surgery, poor wound healing, umbilical stump bleeding in infancy, mucosal bleeding, menorrhagia) and asks about prior surgical/dental bleeding.
Assesses for red flags requiring urgent escalation (e.g., head trauma, neurologic symptoms, severe abdominal pain, heavy uncontrolled bleeding, pregnancy/postpartum bleeding).
Obtains relevant personal and family history (consanguinity, siblings with bleeding, prior miscarriages) and medication history (anticoagulants/antiplatelets, NSAIDs) to contextualize bleeding risk.

Differentiates from more common causes of bleeding with normal PT/aPTT (e.g., von Willebrand disease, platelet disorders, mild hemophilia, connective tissue disorders) and notes that factor XIII deficiency is not detected by PT/aPTT screening.

Communicates a clear plan for confirmatory testing (e.g., factor XIII activity assay; clot solubility test as a screening tool where applicable) and baseline evaluation (CBC, fibrinogen) without claiming they will necessarily be abnormal.
Meets communication milestone explain_diagnosis by including both phrases: 'congenital factor XIII deficiency' and 'normal PT'.

Meets communication milestone explain_treatment by stating first-line therapy is factor XIII concentrate for bleeding/prophylaxis and that rFVIIa may be used as an alternative/rescue option when factor XIII concentrate is unavailable or ineffective.

If discussing any prescribing, performs required safety checks conceptually before prescribing: asks about allergies (critical) and contraindications (critical), and considers drug interactions (non-critical), even if no medication is ultimately prescribed in this L1 task.
Uses patient-centered language, checks understanding, and provides anticipatory guidance (avoid high-risk activities/NSAIDs if appropriate; seek urgent care for significant bleeding or head injury).

Recommend confirmatory testing with factor XIII activity (± clot solubility screening) and hematology involvement; explain treatment options including factor XIII concentrate and rFVIIa as backup.
